<!--
  Describe the biological motivation for this case study. Include relevant 
  explanatory images and references throughout. See this previous case study 
  as an example: 
  https://www.opencasestudies.org/ocs-bp-co2-emissions/#Motivation
  Images or videos may be helpful so this includes an example of including an 
  image.
-->

# **Motivation**

***

Aging is measurable before it is visible. Although we often think of aging in 
terms of clinical frailty, or disease risk, 
{{< glossary "biological age" display="biological aging" >}} begins much earlier 
and unfolds across multiple molecular systems. One of the most reproducible 
correlates of aging is **{{< glossary "DNA methylation" >}}**, a regulatory 
chemical modification that alters gene activity without changing the DNA 
sequence itself.

Over the past decade, researchers have discovered that 
{{< glossary "DNA methylation" display="methylation patterns" >}} at specific 
regions in our DNA change systematically with age across populations, 
environments, and disease states. These observations led to the development of 
**{{< glossary "epigenetic clock" display="epigenetic clocks" >}}**: statistical 
models that estimate {{< glossary "chronological age" >}} directly from 
{{< glossary "methylation profile" display="DNA methylation profiles" >}}. 
The surprising accuracy of these clocks suggests that aging leaves a detectable 
imprint across the {{< glossary genome >}}. Even more intriguingly, deviations 
between {{< glossary "chronological age" display="predicted age" >}} and 
{{< glossary "chronological age" >}} may reflect biological differences in 
health status, environmental exposure, or disease processes.

### What Are Epigenetic Clocks?

:::: {.definition_box}

::: {.definition_box_header}

Definition

:::

::: {.inner_block}

**{{< glossary "epigenetic clock" display="Epigenetic clocks" >}}** are 
statistical or 
{{< glossary "machine learning" display="machine learning models" >}} that 
estimate an individual's {{< glossary "biological age" >}} by learning 
systematic patterns in 
{{< glossary "DNA methylation" display="DNA methylation levels" >}} at specific 
genomic locations ({{< glossary "CpG site" display="CpG sites" >}}). These 
models leverage age-associated changes in 
{{< glossary "DNA methylation" display="methylation" >}} to produce an age 
estimate that can reflect not only chronological time but also aspects of 
{{< glossary "biological age" display="biological aging" >}}, health status, 
and disease risk.

:::

::::

Early {{< glossary "epigenetic clock" display="epigenetic clocks" >}} (like the 
Horvath and Hannum clocks, developed in 2013) were based on 
**{{< glossary "elastic net regression" >}}**, a 
{{< glossary "penalized regression" display="penalized linear regression" >}} 
technique. These models were highly successful because they:

- Handle {{< glossary "high-dimensional data" >}} (many more 
  {{< glossary "CpG site" display="CpGs" >}} than samples)
- Automatically select {{< glossary feature display="predictive features" >}}
- Are computationally efficient
- Generalize reasonably well across tissues and populations

However, biology is complex. 
{{< glossary "DNA methylation" display="Methylation patterns" >}} arise from 
{{< glossary chromatin display="regulatory networks" >}}, 
{{< glossary chromatin display="chromatin structures" >}}, and biological 
{{< glossary interaction display="interactions" >}} that are often 
**{{< glossary nonlinear >}}** processes. Recent work suggests that 
**{{< glossary "deep neural network" display="deep neural networks" >}}** may 
capture patterns that linear models miss by:

- Modeling {{< glossary nonlinear display="nonlinear relationships" >}} 
  among {{< glossary "CpG site" display="CpGs" >}}
- Capturing {{< glossary interaction display="interactions" >}} between 
  genomic regions
- Learning multi-scale patterns across thousands of sites simultaneously

This case study explores how 
{{< glossary "machine learning" display="machine learning models" >}} can learn 
aging signatures from 
{{< glossary "high-dimensional data" display="high-dimensional" >}} 
{{< glossary "DNA methylation" display="DNA methylation data" >}}, beginning 
with the classical 
{{< glossary "elastic net regression" display="elastic net" >}} baseline and 
progressing to 
{{< glossary "deep neural network" display="deep neural networks" >}}.









<!--- EXTRA?                                                                     --- INCORPORATE THIS?




This case study explores how machine learning models can learn those aging 
signatures from high-dimensional DNA methylation data. We begin with classical 
penalized regression models and then move toward deep neural networks capable of 
capturing nonlinear structure across thousands of DNA regions simultaneously.

DNA methylation-based *epigenetic clocks* have transformed aging research by 
providing quantitative markers of biological age that often outperform 
chronological age in predicting morbidity, frailty, and mortality. Although 
most established clocks to date rely on penalized linear regression (e.g., 
elastic net), recent works have demonstrated that deep learning may capture 
richer nonlinear relationships among DNA methylation sites, improving 
predictive accuracy.

This case study walks learners through reconstructing a neural network 
epigenetic clock model in the `R` statistical programming language. Learners 
will use `scorcher`, an `R` package designed for developing deep learning 
models with ease. The goal is to give the learners a hands-on experience 
working with DNA methylation data, introduce epigenetic clock models, teach 
modern neural network construction in `R`, and highlight practical modeling 
issues such as overfitting, interpretability, and biological validation.


[Images are very helpful within this section....]{style="color: red;"}

[NEED IMAGE]{style="color: red;"} <!-- ![](img/motivation_map_of_monitors.jpg){fig-alt="Map showing location of air pollution monitors across the US." width=800 .lightbox} -->

***
